entry.title: Supply Protection (SUP) in aATP - SAP S4 HANA
entry.content:
Primarily, the objective of supply protection is to safeguard inventory quantities and prioritize inventory for incoming demand. Implementing supply protection allows the user to ensure that the essential supplies are assigned to key customer groups or requirements based on the chosen key characteristics.

For example, an organization has three distribution channels for merchandise: E-Commerce (Online), Wholesale, and Retail. Let’s assume that the sales margin for merchandise distributed via e-commerce is significantly higher than through the other distribution channels. During the Christmas season, the business may want to prioritize its e-commerce distribution while ensuring that the other channels run smoothly. In the sales order management process, orders are typically created in sequential order without considering any prioritization. Supply Protection helps by safeguarding inventory for prioritized needs, such as the higher-margin E-Commerce channel. This means that quantities reserved for E-Commerce will not be available for other channels when product availability is checked during sales order creation. Let’s dive into details with SAP terminologies.

Certain terminologies are essential for understanding the core concept of supply protection as they are used in the system configuration and  setup for fashion and retail articles:

  • Supply Protection SUP: This is a method for safeguarding certain quantities of a material at a specific plant against other demands. Protection groups are established based on defined criteria and can be assigned different priorities.
  • Supply Protection Object: This component is essential for enabling supply protection during product availability checks. A supply protection object is designated for a single material-plant combination, but multiple supply protection objects can be managed for the same material-plant combination.
  • Core (Horizontal) Protection: This type of supply protection aims to prevent different customer groups from accessing each other’s inventory. For instance, if the goal is to protect markets such as Germany and the UK, when a sales order is created for the German market, the quantity reserved for the UK will not be available for Germany or any other group.
  • Prioritized (Vertical) Protection: This type of supply protection is used to allocate quantities based on various characteristics and their associated priorities. For example, it might protect distribution channels like e-commerce, wholesale, or retail. When a sales order is generated for the e-commerce channel, the quantities reserved for e-commerce will not be available for wholesale or any other channel.
  • Protected Quantity: It refers to the amount of material set aside for a specific group, which must fulfill the demands of groups with equal or lower priority. Supply protection consistently utilizes the base unit of measure established in the material master record. This unit is used to define both protected and consumed quantities during the Available to Promise (ATP) check.
  • Consumed Quantity: This is the quantity of confirmed sales orders or stock transport orders that diminishes the remaining protected inventory (refer to “remaining protection” for further details).
  • Remaining Protection: This is the sum of the unconsumed and unexpired protected quantities across individual time buckets. For instance, if there are three time buckets, each protecting 200 pieces, and 500 pieces are consumed in the second bucket, the remaining protected quantity would total 400 pieces (200 pieces from bucket 1 and 200 pieces from bucket 3). The planned protected quantity remains at 600 pieces, while the consumed quantity is 500 pieces. Remaining protection is calculated for each time bucket, protection group, and supply protection object. A similar calculation is conducted for both consumption and protection.
  • Planning Period/Horizon: This refers to the time period during which protection data is maintained. The period is determined by the planning timeframe and the specified time intervals (such as weekly, monthly, etc.), which generate the time buckets.

Note: You might wonder why there are two protection objects–horizontal and vertical–when the protection objective could be achieved with just one (either horizontal or vertical). The reason lies in the potential complexity of protection requirements.

For example, the objective may be to safeguard distribution for specific distribution channels like e-commerce, wholesale, or retail for a specific market like Germany or the UK. When a sales document is generated for the e-commerce channel with the sold-to party in Germany, the reserved quantity for Germany’s e-commerce is unavailable. This quantity is also restricted for wholesale channels in Germany or any other combination of horizontal and vertical protection.

To illustrate this, consider the following matrix:

As shown in the matrix, protection for wholesale demand from the US is 1,500, which must be respected by the wholesale demand from the UK. Similarly, the wholesale demand from the UK is protected at 3,000 units, which must be respected by the wholesale demand from the US.

Abbreviation explanations:

  • PRT for W/S - Protection for Wholesale distribution
  • PRT for RTL - Protection for Retail distribution
  • PRT for E-com - Protection for E-com distribution
  • Unre. St - Unrestricted/ unprotected stock
  • R. Qty - Required Quantity

As per the above illustration, 9000 & 4000 pieces have been protected for e-commerce and wholesale channels, respectively. Even though the requirement is 3,500 pieces, only the protected quantity for the retail channel is utilized. The remaining quantity is taken from the unprotected/unrestricted stock.

Note: Once the planning horizon expires, the protection object becomes inactive. As a result, all protections (vertical and horizontal) associated with that protection object become irrelevant.

The primary application you will use to create the protection object is as follows:

App Name: Manage Supply Protection/ App Id: F4569

Currently, there is no specific app for mass maintenance of supply protection objects. Refer to: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/15f9e51998a945cc82545cb6b4dbe5c2/ced8d281241040358bc54ba57baae01d.html

Prerequisite:

  • Supply Protection Activation in the availability check. (The availability check that you have maintained particularly in the article (For the relevant DC).)
  • Protection Object maintenance. (Check for the above information) 

Supported Document Types

The active supply protection supports the following document types:

  • Sales documents
  • Stock transport orders
  • Contracts
  • Scheduling agreements
  • Deliveries free of charge

When setting up the vertical and horizontal protections, if you cannot find the characteristics needed to protect your supply, the Manage Characteristics Catalog app allows you to customize the characteristics during the protection object creation. It is also important to understand the significant difference between Supply Protection and Product Allocation. Please reach out to me for a detailed explanation on this regard.

 
Useful Links: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/f132c385e0234fe68ae9ff35b2da178c/c4b704762cbd4611a3ee2dc00c7a7277.html

entry.title: [SAP] Enterprise Structure – Definition and Assignment Map
Start with new business process - the Advanced Intercompany Sales(5D2)
entry.content:
This is blog provides an overview for new standard process - the Advanced Intercompany Sales. The Advanced  Intercompany sales processes allow two affiliated companies and an external customer to participate in a single intercompany process. 

First, let’s assume a business scenario where a multinational apparel group owns company A domestically, which only sells but does not actually produce clothes. The group also owns another company B abroad, which has factories to handle the actual production of clothing. When a customer orders clothing from company A, company A makes an internal purchase to company B, and eventually the clothing is physically shipped to the customer from company B. For this kind of business scenario, we can consider to use the intercompany sales process. And with the arrival of SAP S/4HANA 2022, there comes the advanced intercompany sales process (Scope Item 5D2). Refer to the What's New in SAP S/4HANA 2022 for more info: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/f5d3e1005efd4e86acf9a65abf428082/ce3d603f0ba6434fb908318344965912.html?version=2022.000

The process for advanced intercompany sales provides functions that allow two affiliated companies to participate in a single intercompany process, such as the following:

  • A local sales office sells to a customer. The producing company delivers the goods directly to the customer.
  • A local sales organization sells to a customer. A central warehouse delivers the goods.

The following graphic shows a simplified overview of the process for advanced intercompany sales and let's take it over into the above assumed scenario:
A multinational apparel group has a domestic company 1010, which only sells but does not actually produce clothing, which we call the selling company. The group also owns another company abroad 1710, which has a factory 1710 that is responsible for the actual production of clothing, which we call the delivering company.

  1. the sales organization belonging to selling company 1010 orders goods by creating a standard sales order from plant 1710, which is indeed belongs to delivering company 1710.
  2. to keep the postings synchronized for financial and material ledger purposes, the system creates an intercompany purchase order in the selling company 1010 for the intercompany purchase to the delivering company 1710. This intercompany purchase order then generates an intercompany sales order in the delivering company 1710.
  3. the delivering company 1710 is responsible for the physical delivery of the goods to the customer.
  4. the delivering company 1710 issues an intercompany customer invoice for the selling company 1010 and the selling company 1010 issues a customer invoice for the external customer.

A standard sales order can initiate the entire process, which is integrated into the value chain monitoring framework. To orchestrate and monitor each process step, this business processes makes use of the Monitor Value Chains app. For more information, see Value Chain Monitoring. For the transfer of control of goods between the affiliated companies and to the customer, the system uses valuated stock in transit. For more information, see Valuated Stock in Transit.

Advanced intercompany sales process vs. classical intercompany sales process

The classical intercompany sales process is simple and there are only few business documents between affiliated companies. But with the increasing needs for business, audits and finance, the classical intercompany sales processes are no longer enough to meet current requirement. The new advanced intercompany sales process brings more intercompany business documents between affiliated companies. The use of the Value Chain Monitoring Framework (VCM) enables most business documents to be generated automatically by the system, effectively reducing the additional efforts on end users while increasing number of business documents.

New documents adding:

Comparing to the classcial intercompany sales processes, the new advanced intercompany sales process adds the following business documents:
For the selling company:

  • Intercompany purchase order
  • Intercompany supplier invoice
  • Stock transfer to valuated stock in transit

For the delivering company:

  •  Intercompany sales order
  • Stock transfer to valuated stock in transit

A completely new framework and Fiori applications

The Advanced Intercompany Sales process uses the Value Chain Monitoring Framework (VCM) and brings a new Fiori application, Monitor Value Chains - App ID F4854. Refer to the following link for more detailed info about this fiori app:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F4854') 

This fiori app can be used to monitor various types of value chain related processes, including Advanced Intercompany Sales as described in this blog, as well as other processes such as Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer and so on. The application shows all the process steps and business documents involved in the relevant process. This app also connects with other fiori apps, allowing users to navigate to the corresponding apps to view or change relevant business documents.

Prerequisites for Advanced Intercompany Sales

To set up system for the use of the Advanced Intercompany Sales process either by executing all steps in the configuration guide attached to SAP Note 3233830 or by activating the process with scope item 5D2 in one content-based system. This process is supported by features of the Monitor Value Chains app.

The configuration steps inside SAP Note 3233830 involve multiple configurations for multiple function modules, all of which need to be configured correctly for the process to work properly. To troubleshooting problems caused by configuration errors, SAP note 3390467 provides a report 'VCM_CHECK' to checks relevant configurations within system. Another SAP note 3366067 documents the current restrictions and general Information for Advanced Intercompany Sales.

To monitor the execution of the entire process, the fiori application Monitor Value Chains should be used. End users shoud have the required authorizations to ensure that they can use this fiori application properly. For that, extending custom business roles to include the business catalog ‘SAP_CA_BC_VCM_MONITOR’ or directly using the standard delivered business roles, such as, Shipping Specialist (SAP_BR_SHIPPING_SPECIALIST). For more details, check link: https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F4854')/S24OP

Usage of the App "Monitor Value Chains"

The Process Flow tab in the app provides complete process step and business document information to help track the flow and the execution status of process steps.

The Message Log in this application can provide detailed errors reported for better troubleshooting. After resolving the error, use the "Reprocess" button to retrigger the execution of business processes again, especially those business document creation steps that need to be performed automatically by the system.

For more detailed information about this new business process, please check Help Portal:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/7b24a64d9d0941bda1afa753263d9e39/6c2684455628425fbc5db2d0f0721f31.html?version=2022.000

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Functionality for EDI Processing: Setup and Testing of API for Inbound Order Creation
entry.content:
From IDOCs to APIs

 

 

In my previous posts, I described various aspects of EDI inbound order processing using the classical IDOC-based approach. However, SAP considers IDOCs an outdated technology, and the use of APIs is recommended for S/4HANA implementations. The purpose of this article is to demonstrate how a functional consultant can select and test an API for sales order creation.

This article explains how to select an API and test a SOAP API using simple requests directly with system master data. The next article will cover mapping external data, such as customer partner and material numbers, GLNs, EAN/GTINs, and sales area determination.

How to select API for Sales order creation: B2B API

Let's start from selection of API for Sales order creation for SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Private Edition. The starting point for searching for APIs is the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

By selecting the APIs section and filtering by the product SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, you will find a wide range of more or less relevant results. The most important factor for selection is the purpose coded as A2A, A2X, and B2B. It’s also essential to note whether the API is marked as ODATA or SOAP, as this affects the implementation and testing technique. However, I believe the purpose is the primary criterion for selection, while the API type should be considered secondarily.

api.sap.com

A2X, A2A, or B2B - What to Select for EDI Interchange?

The SAP Help Portal provides the following explanation of these API types in the APIs for Sales article:

  • A2X stands for application to X users. You can use an A2X service to connect SAP S/4HANA with another system in your system landscape. It is an OData-based web service with a synchronous execution.
  • A2A stands for application to application. You can use an A2A service to connect SAP S/4HANA with another system in your system landscape. It is a SOAP-based web service with an asynchronous execution.
  • B2B stands for business to business. You can use a B2B service to connect SAP S/4HANA with a system in another system landscape. It is a SOAP-based web service with an asynchronous execution.

Based on this description, I conclude that for EDI purpose, B2B is the more applicable option.

Indeed, if you compare body structure of A2X and B2B, you will find the difference. B2B provides wide options for external data, such as GLNs, external partner numbers, EAN/GTINSs, Customer material info records (CMIR) and so on. Alternatively, A2X looks like a reflection of SAP standard tables and dedicated to work with internal SAP identifiers. 

B2B vs A2X: partner details

Based on the explanation above, I selected the "Sales Order/Customer Return - Create, Update, Cancel (B2B)" API.

Basic information about this API is available on the API page of SAP Business Accelerator Hub. The Documentation tab on the API page provides a link to help.sap.com , where additional details can be found, including configuration steps and sample payloads. The technical name of this service is ORDERREQUEST_IN, which will be needed later.

Setup for Testing: AIF

The SOAP API OrderRequest_In uses the SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) for processing and monitoring. To begin, ensure that the scenario ID SAP_COM_0223 is deployed as described in the relevant section of SAP Help.

Checking Deployment

To verify if the scenario is already deployed:

  1. Use the monitoring transaction code /AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling .
    As with all transaction codes starting with /, run it as /n/AIF/ERR from the Command field or add it to your Favorites.
  2. Check if /SDEDI Sales & Distribution EDI Integration is available in the Namespace search help. If it is not available, you will need to deploy it.
    SAP "Monitoring and Error Handling" screen with '/SDEDI' namespace for Sales & Distribution EDI Integration.

Sales & Distribution EDI Integration Deployment

To deploy the required content:

  1. Use transaction code /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT (AIF Content Transport – Deploy).
  2. Add the deployment scenario ID SAP_COM_0223.
  3. Press F8 (Execute) to complete the deployment.

"AIF Content Transport" screen for deploying scenario ID SAP_COM_0223.

Confirmation of Successful Deployment:

Deployment Scenario SAP_COM_0223 successfully extracted.

AIF Licensing

There has been some discussion in the community about whether AIF requires additional licensing. Based on my research, AIF services provided within the SAP namespace do not require extra licensing. In this case, the service falls under the /SDEDI namespace, so no separate licensing is necessary.

However, it is recommended to confirm this with the responsible person in your organization for complete assurance.

SOAP API Testing Using SE80 Object Navigator

A common method for testing API is sending requests to the SAP system using tools like Postman. This option mimics real interchange but requires additional effort to learn new tools and address potential infrastructure challenges such as connectivity and access. For processing a payload by the SAP S/4HANA system without infrastructure issues, a simpler option is testing directly within the system using SE80 Object Navigator.

Run t-code SE80 Object Navigator. If Enterprise Service Browser is unavailable, enable it: Utilities > Settings > Workbench (General) > Enterprise Services Browser.

Enabling Enterprise Services Browser in SE80 ABAP Workbench settings under Utilities > Settings.

Please select Enterprise Services Browser and open the Web Service Definition ORDERREQUEST_IN.

SE80: Accessing ORDERREQUEST_IN in Enterprise Services Browser under Web Service Definition.

Press F8 to test.

SE80: Displaying OrderRequest_In service definition details for creating, changing, or deleting sales orders.

Notice: Another available option is to navigate through: Repository Browser > Package > LOG_EDI_INTEGRATION > Subpackages > LOG_EDI_SD > Subpackages > LOG_EDI_SD_IMPL_ORDER > Enterprise Services > Service Definitions > ORDERREQUEST_IN.

SE80: Detailed view of OrderRequest_In service definition within LOG_EDI_INTEGRATION package.

Select Input Template

  1. Request template: informative but complex
  2. Template without payload: easy to paste your payload

SE80: Test Service Provider screen for ORDER_REQUEST_IN with options to generate a request template.

Example below is a template without payload. Switch to Editor (1) to paste or edit text, or Load an existing test file (2)

SE80: Test Service Provider: Display Request screen showing XML template for OrderRequest structure.

It is also possible to save and open payloads using the Save variant and Get variant options.

SE80: Save variant and Get variant.

When Request is prepared by Editor or loaded, following features are helpful:

  1. Execute request
  2. Check XML
  3. Download the request for future use
  4. Adjust XML for better readability

SE80: XML Request editor

Run the request:  F8 Execute. A notice indicates successful processing for this API, meaning that the sales order has been created.

Successful with empty result - commit may be required.

Run VA03 to find a created Sales Order. The last order created by the user will be used as the Order number. Alternatively, use VA05 to find created Orders. More monitoring options are in the next section. The error response below indicates a request with the same PO number was previously sent.

SE80: XML Response with Error Description

Basic Payload Example

When starting testing, I prefer to use a simple example based on an existing sales order in the system. Testing with a clear and straightforward case makes debugging easier, minimizing references to EDI-specific logic.

To create such an example:

  1. Identify an Existing Sales Order: Select a sales order already present in the system.
  2. Recreate It Manually: Ensure the settings and master data are valid by manually recreating the order.
  3. Generate Payload: Use a simple template to create the payload based on this verified sales order.

Below is an example of a basic order payload. This payload contains the minimal required data to create a sales order using system data directly, without any mapping. 

  • Message Header Data: Mandatory but can use any placeholder values for basic testing.
  • Order Data: Must be populated with relevant data, such as details from an existing sales order.

Additionally, I included comments within the payload. These comments can be excluded as needed. This example is particularly useful for training purposes or for simulating intercompany scenarios where both systems involved in the interchange use the same master data.

Order Request Payload

 

 

 

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <InternalID>DummyS</InternalID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_1</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-10-23</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <!-- Order type and Sales area-->
  <SalesDocumentType>ZOR</SalesDocumentType>
  <SalesOrganization>1000</SalesOrganization>
  <DistributionChannel>10</DistributionChannel>
  <OrganizationDivision>00</OrganizationDivision>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <!-- SAP number of Sold-to party-->
   <SupplierPartyID>211</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <!-- SAP number of Ship-to party-->
   <SupplierPartyID>212</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">1.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <!-- SAP number of material-->
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
   </Product>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

 

This basic example provides a minimal setup for creating a sales order while using direct system data. Feel free to adjust the values based on your specific scenario or testing needs.

Let’s test using SE80, as explained above: prepare an payload and Press F8 Execute:

Simple XML Order Request

Result:

Processing result

VA03 Display sales order: Highlighted fields, such as those populated from the payload data, including Sold-To Party, Ship-To Party, and Order Type, are displayed. Other fields, like Payment Terms, Incoterms, and Item Description, are automatically populated based on the system's master data and configuration settings.

VA03 Sales order

It’s important to note that the master data must be sufficiently complete. For example, if Incoterms or Payment Terms are missing from the customer master record, an error may occur during processing. To address this issue, you can either update the master data to include the missing information or add the required data directly to the payload.

Extended Payload Example

This is another “simple” example that directly uses system data without relying on EDI-specific settings. However, it demonstrates several important EDI-related features:

  • OrderItemText: If provided, this field replaces the text from the material master data.
  • BuyerProductID: Even if not used for mapping, it is stored in the related item field and can be utilized for outputs.
  • ExpectedNetPrice: This value is mapped to the EDI1 price condition.
  • ExpectedNetAmount: This value is mapped to the EDI2 price condition.

 

 

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <InternalID>DummyS</InternalID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_1.1</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-10-23</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <SalesDocumentType>ZOR</SalesDocumentType>
  <SalesOrganization>1000</SalesOrganization>
  <DistributionChannel>10</DistributionChannel>
  <OrganizationDivision>00</OrganizationDivision>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <SupplierPartyID>211</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <SupplierPartyID>212</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>CustMatID</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

Sales order:

VA03 Sales Order: customer material information

VA03: Sales order, Customer expected Price and Value

Monitoring of SOAP API Processing

Since SOAP API processing is based on the Application Interface Framework (AIF), AIF-related tools can be used for monitoring and error handling.

/AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling. To improve performance and reduce the volume of data displayed, apply the following filters on the selection screen:

  • Application: AIF
  • Namespace: /SDEDI

Additionally, adjust the status selection to include both error and successful messages to gain a comprehensive view of the processing result

/AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling: Selection screen

When you run the report, messages are displayed in a structured format, organized by Sender ID (from the message header) and PO number.

  • To view details, select a message from the list.
  • The log records will display information about either successful processing or error occurrences, providing clarity on the message status and any required actions.

/AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling: ALV screen

/AIF/LOG - Interface Logs. Another available option for monitoring. To narrow down the results and generate a focused report, select a relevant Subobject during the selection process.

/AIF/LOG - Interface Logs: Selection screen.

Select message on the upper screen and find the log records on the lower screen.

/AIF/LOG - Interface Logs: ALV screen

The transactions SRT_MONI (WS Message Monitor) and SRT_UTIL (Web Service Utilities) are frequently mentioned in the context of SOAP monitoring. However they are dedicated for lower transmission infrastructure level and not relevant for payload processing errors and monitoring of messages created by SE80 test technique.

Handling Errors

Successfully processing an inbound message on the first attempt is very rare. Typically, it requires an iterative process of running the message, adjusting the payload, and refining the system setup or master data.

SOAP API processing provides informative error messages as part of the response to help you identify and resolve issues. Below are some common errors and their possible causes.

Typical Errors and Their Causes

Class II_EDI_ORDER_REQUEST_IN is not a proxy class. This error appears if AIF deployment scenario hasn’t been extracted.

Error response: Class II_EDI_ORDER_REQUEST_IN is not a proxy class.

The Source Party internal ID must be provided in the source data. Message header sender data is not complete.

Error response: The Source Party internal ID must be provided in the source data.

Sales area cannot be determined. This occurs when key parameters are missing: Sales Order Type, Sales Organization, Distribution Channel, or Division. Ensure these are provided or configured for determination by external data.

Error response: Sales area cannot be determined.

Supplier Party ID 0000001111 is not found. This occurs when the provided Supplier Party ID does not match the system's master data. Ensure the ID is correct and exists in the system.

Error response: Supplier Party ID 0000001111 is not found.

Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined. This occurs when Supplier party ID is missing, and Buyer party ID isn’t configured for external determination.

Error response: Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined.

Material 1521-00 is not defined for sales org.1000, distr.chan.10, language EN. This occurs when the provided Supplier Product ID does not match the system's master data.

Error response: Material 1521-00 is not defined for sales org. 1000, distr. chan. 10, language EN.

Acknowledgements and System Overview

I would like to thank my colleagues, Askar Timofeev and Vadim Vologzhanin, for helping me learn this topic. I also extend my gratitude to Capgemini for providing a sandbox system, which I used for investigations, creating test examples, and preparing screenshots. The sandbox was SAP S/4HANA 2022 (S/4HANA On-Premise Release 2022 SP 01, February 2023 FPS).

entry.title: Flexible Item Category Determination in SAP Cloud Public - Custom Adaptions
entry.content:
Why Flexible Item Category Determination Matters!

As Sales Configuration Experts, we all know that the Item categories control how sales document items behave and thus meet different demands in the business processes.

However, the limitations of the Sales Public Cloud prevent the creation of Z-Sales Document Types. As a result, we are unable to control the determination of the appropriate item category that will control the item behavior in the sales documents. Consequently, most users are required to make manual changes when processing these documents.

Flexible Determination

Using the Flexible Item Category Determination, The system can evaluate specific business conditions and propose an item category according to your business rules instead of the Default Item Category assignment.

Example:

Your client needs to manage the behavior of business processes by selecting the appropriate Order Reason. For a certain type of orders, a down payment is required for all products. Therefore, the client will select "Down Payment" from the dropdown list. Once this selection is made, the system can propose the CBAO item category when specific business conditions outlined in a decision table are met, allowing the billing plan to be automatically enabled.

Define the following Business Rule to Your Decision Table.

 

Sales Document Type Item Category Group Item Category Usage Item Category of Higher-Level Item SD Order Reason Item Category

 

= OR= NORM<empty><empty>= 01 (Down Payment)= CBAO

Note: If the user doesn't select any order reason or any available alternatives, the system finds no logic in your decision table. The system proposes the default item category TAN.

Step-by-Step Guide to Configuration

Step 1: Create PRFplus Objects

you can create Business Rule Framework Plus (BRFplus) applications, functions, and expressions from templates.

You perform this in the configuration activity Create BRFplus Objects for Sales Document Categories.

  • Custom Adaption: ITEM_CATEGORY_DETERMINATITEION

 

  • Select Document Category: C (Orders) And Press Enter

     

A BRFplus application template is provided. The application template includes a function template and an expression template.

 

  • Press The Copy Button to Enter the PRFplus Object (PRFplus Application)  

     

     

  • Save Your Configuration
  • Navigate Simplified BRFplus Workbench app and Configure your Business Rules.

     

  • In the following Application you can define the new Business Rules to your Decision Table.
    • Find your application in the repository.
    • Expand the application node, the expression node, and then the decision table node.
    • Choose the decision table to display it.
    • Click Edit

 

 

 

  • Now you can add a row to your decision table and add values in cells as shown below.

     

  • You can now check, save and activate Your Business Rules.
     
  • Recommendation

    To prevent any inconsistencies between your business rules and the existing item category assignments in the Assign Item Categories configuration activity, choose values for the following conditions and the result by using the search help:

    • Sales document type
    • Item category group
    • Item category of higher-level item
    • Item usage
    • Item category

     Results

    The implementation is complete for the sales document category to which the activated BRFplus application, function, and expression are assigned. The flexible item category determination is enabled for sales documents that belong to the sales document category. 

Testing the Configuration

  1. Create Standard Sales Order And Choose the Order Reason defined in your Business Rule.

 

Thanks!

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Functionality for EDI Processing: Inbound Order Creation by API vs. IDOC (Part 1)
entry.content:
In my previous post, I described how to select an API for creating orders based on EDI data and how to test a payload using SE80. This article focuses on a key aspect of EDI integration: mapping external partners to internal partners and determining the sales area. The mapping of external material numbers to internal ones will be covered in the next part. My goal is to compare whether the API supports the features available for IDocs, identify any new opportunities, and highlight deviations that require consideration.

The purpose of this analysis is to evaluate whether the selected API can replace IDoc-based processing. To achieve this, I examine the logic that aligns closely with classical IDocs. While the API may offer extended functionality, such cases are beyond the scope of this article.

As mentioned in my previous post, the appropriate API for inbound order creation is ORDERREQUEST_IN: Sales Order/Customer Return - Create, Update, Cancel (B2B). This API is compared with the IDoc ORDERS processing, which uses process code ORDE and function module IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS.

TLDR: Main Comparison Results

The B2B API supports most of the IDoc logic in the described areas while introducing several useful new features:

  • The B2B API ORDERREQUEST_IN is not a wrapper for IDoc processing but a separate implementation.
  • Partner determination uses EDPAR, similar to IDoc processing.
  • New feature: Partner determination by GLN, based on partner master data.
  • Order type and sales area determination use EDSDC. Simplified logic is recommended, though legacy logic is also supported.
  • Material determination employs similar logic for vendor material and customer material info records.
  • New feature: Material and UoM determination by EAN/GTIN, based on material master data.
  • Legacy logic for EAN/GTIN based on VB12 is not supported, and additional cross-checks have been introduced in the determination logic.

For a detailed explanation of partner and sales area determination, refer to the following sections. This article is extensive, so the detailed description of material determination will be published in a subsequent post.

 

API ORDERREQUEST_IN: Technical Details

I could not find any clear and detailed documentation of the business logic for this API. Therefore, most of the information was derived through testing and analyzing the code. From a technical perspective, the ORDERREQUEST_IN API is not a wrapper for IDoc-related code; it is implemented as separate logic.

My investigation focused on the methods within the LOG_EDI_SD_IMPL_ORDER package. The logic is distributed across multiple classes, and in some cases, the comments within the methods provide valuable insights. For example, as shown below, the MAP_PRODUCT method in the CL_EDI_SD_ORDR_MAPPING class contains a sufficient description of its logic.

MAP_PRODUCT: material mapping logic for EDI in SD.

Partner Determination Logic

For illustration, I will use the same organizational model example as in the article about Partner Determination for Inbound Orders .

Your company is a supplier, and you have the following customer:

ACME Group is a large multi-division company operating across various markets. They leverage economies of scale by using a group entity to manage their EDI operations. In your market, they are represented by the legal entity ACME Corp, and you ship goods to their distribution centre, ACME DC.

ACME sends sales orders through their EDI provider, which converts the messages and forwards them to your EDI provider. Your provider then translates these messages into API requests for processing in your SAP system. As it is traditional for Europe, ACME uses GLNs (Global Location Numbers) for communication and business parties. Additional details about their GLNs are as follows:

Master Records in the Supplier's SAP ERP

  • ACME Group: GLN 7089900000009 - 246
  • ACME Corp: GLN 7089900000016 - 211
  • ACME DC: GLN 7089900000023 - 212

EDI flow: buyer to seller via providers

To create a sales order in the SAP system, external partners must be mapped to SAP master data as follows:

  • Sender → SAP Group Customer
  • Buyer → SAP Sold-to Party
  • Delivery Point → SAP Ship-to Party
  • Supplier → SAP Sales Organization and Distribution Channel

Partner Determination: Sender

The sender partner is specified in the  <MessageHeader> section, outside of the Order segments. I consider it a communication partner, as opposed to business partners like Sold-to and Ship-to.

Example of XML structure:

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
<MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
    <InternalID>246</InternalID>
    <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
</MessageHeader>

 

Based on the XML structure, two types of IDs can be used for sender determination:

  • InternalID
  • StandardID

The InternalID is the preferred value and will be processed regardless of the presence of the StandardID. Technically, it can be any dummy value, which will be visible in AIF monitoring. As mentioned in my previous article, any value is acceptable if business partners are provided as internal SAP numbers. However, this value is critical for EDPAR determination. In the EDPAR table, there is a validation to ensure that the partner number exists as a business partner. As a result, it is recommended to use a relevant partner number. In my example, I use customer 246, which represents an SAP group customer.

If the InternalID is not provided, the StandardID will be processed instead. According to comments in SAP program code, a GLN number is expected here (not a DUNS number, which would be useful for the US market).

SAP code includes the following comment:

 

*---> Only 1 standard ID should be sent (GLN) - DUNS is not supported
*---> If there is one, we assume it is a GLN

 

Partner determination is based on the GLN from customer master data. If a unique partner exists with the provided GLN, the corresponding SAP partner number will be determined and processed further. Otherwise, processing will fail.

In comparison, for IDOC processing, sender determination requires the setup of partner profiles using transaction code WE20 (Partner Profiles). These can be configured as either KU (Customer) or LS (Logical System), with different logic applied in the associated mappings. However, in the case of IDOC, an intermediate entity like the WE20 Partner Profile is not required.

Sender Partner Error Handling

Any value is applicable for InternalID, but it must match the EDPAR settings if these are in use. If the internal sender ID is incorrect and does not match the EDPAR settings, an error will occur during business partner determination. Business partner determination, which operates based on SAP internal numbers or the GLN from master data, is not affected by the sender ID.

If StandardID is used and the sender partner cannot be determined from the provided GLN (e.g., if it is missing or not unique), the error message is self-explanatory but does not specify the partner type in question.

GLN error: Unable to uniquely determine business partner in SAP EDI.

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
  <standard>
    <faultText> Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
    <faultDetail>
      <severity>error</severity>
      <text>Business partner cannot be uniquely determined by GLN 7089900000099</text>
      <id>013(EDI_SD)</id>
    </faultDetail>
  </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

Partner Determination: Business Partners

Business partner information is stored in the Order/Party segment, and the basic determination logic is described in the code as follows:

 

*---> There are 3 possible ways to determine the party internal ID:
*1) If the supplier Party ID is sent, it is taken as the internal party ID 
*   - If incorrect → error
*2) If the buyer Party ID is sent, it is used to determine the internal party *ID 
*   - If incorrect → error
*3) If the global location number (GLN) is sent, it is used to determine the *internal party ID 
*   - GLN is retrieved from the BP master record

 

Similar to IDOC processing, the internal SAP number is the first option, followed by the external ID, which is determined using the EDPAR table mapping. However, unlike IDOC processing, an additional GLN step is included, leveraging the partner master data. Based on my experience, it was the most desired option to use GLN from customer master data, without any mapping tables!

For IDOC processing, my recommendation is to use the GLN as an external number and map it to SAP numbers via the EDPAR table (transaction VOE4) or, if the GLN is not provided, use the external partner number. For this API logic I see three reasonable options to consider:

  • GLN from master data (GlobalLocationNumber field) if the master data quality is sufficient for unique determination.
  • GLN as an external number, with additional maintenance effort but ensuring robust and separate mapping. In this case, the GLN must be treated as the BuyerPartyID.
  • External number (GlobalLocationNumber) if the GLN is not provided.

According to the code, sequential determination is not supported. For example, if the BuyerPartyID is provided but determination fails, the logic does not attempt to use the GLN, even if it is available.

While mapping the BuyerPartyID to SAP numbers uses the same EDPAR table, SAP provides a dedicated section in SPRO for SOAP API-related settings:
SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Electronic Data Interchange > EDI Messages > Convert External to Internal Partner Numbers.
I do not see any functional difference between using this SPRO option and the VOE4 (Sales EDI Conversion) transaction.

IMG configuration: External-to-internal partner number mapping in SAP EDI.

Let’s explore Business Partner Determination using examples of Sold-to and Ship-to determination with my test payloads.

Business Partner Determination by external partner numbers

The Party segment contains the Party type qualifier (PartyType) and the External Partner ID (BuyerPartyID). The GLN (Global Location Number) may also be provided in the GlobalLocationNumber field; however, if the BuyerPartyID is provided, the GLN will always be ignored. The qualifiers SoldTo and ShipTo are self-explanatory.

Here is a part of a test payload example:

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_201124_3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
    <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
    <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
    <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
    <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
</Order>

 

To process such payloads, the following EDPAR settings are required:

EDPAR table: Mapping external partners to internal numbers in SAP.

In this case, 246 is the sender number, provided directly in SenderParty > InternalID or determined by the GLN specified in SenderParty > StandardID. The logic functions similarly to the Customer as Sender logic used for IDOCs.

I am testing this using SE80, as explained in my previous article.

API test request: XML structure for EDI order creation in SAP.

Order request example:

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-23T02:58:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
<InternalID>246</InternalID>
   <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_01</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-22</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header Note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material Sales Text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

 

 

The result of processing can be found in /AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling. The sender partner number (246) and the customer PO number are used as nodes in the tree structure.

EDI error log: Key messages for inbound sales order processing.

The sales order created by this request is:

Standard Order: Overview of sales order created via EDI.

Business Partner Determination by GLNs

In this second example, I will demonstrate how the determination logic works based on GLN numbers stored in the partner master record (General Data > Identification).

Business Partner: Identification with GLN and check digit in SAP.

In the payload example below, the Sender, Sold-to, and Ship-to are determined by their respective GLNs:

API request XML: EDI order details for testing in SAP.

Order request example, GLN partners

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-22T03:58:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_01_GLN</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-22</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header Note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material Sales Text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

Created sales order:

Standard Order: Overview of sales order created via EDI (GLN based determination)

Business Partner Determination: Error Handling

If an error occurs during partner determination, the fault description includes an explanation of which step failed.

For example, if partner determination fails during the step based on BuyerPartyID, the response will be as follows:

Error: Sold-to party role not determined in SAP EDI response.

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global"                        xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined</text>
            <id>003(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

If only the GlobalLocationNumber is provided and the business partner cannot be determined from the given GLN (e.g., if it is missing or not unique), the error message is self-explanatory but does not specify the partner type in question.

Error response: GLN ambiguity prevents business partner determination in EDI.

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" 
                         xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Business partner cannot be uniquely determined by GLN 7089900000096</text>
            <id>013(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

Order Type and Sales Area Determination Logic

When creating a sales order in the SAP system, the order type and sales organization data must be determined. SAP developers aimed to implement reasonable logic while considering legacy logic previously used, such as for IDOC processing. Some insights and reflections on this can be found in the program code comments.

Although sales area data assignment uses the same EDSDC table, SAP provides a dedicated section in SPRO for SOAP API-related settings:
SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Electronic Data Interchange > EDI Messages > Assign Customer/Supplier to Sales Organization Data.

I do not see any significant differences between using the SPRO option and transaction VOE2 (Sales EDI Customer/Supplier).

IMG: Assign customer/supplier to sales organization data in SAP EDI.

Several options are provided for determination logic. Let’s review the following option:

Order Type and Sales area determination by BuyerPartyID of Supplier Party 

According to the code comments, the recommended approach is to assign the Sold-to SAP customer number and the Supplier Party BuyerPartyID to the required order type and sales area.

 

*---> In the new standard case the BuyerPartyID of the Supplier Party (LF) is used to determine the sales area and document type.
*     This is the prefered option from now on.

 

Payload example (Sold-to is determined by EDPAR table):

 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_201124_3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>

 

 

  • The EDPAR table is used to map the BuyerPartyID to the SAP customer number. This record must be created for Sold-to determination.

EDPAR table: Sold-to party mapping from external to internal number.

  • The EDSDC table is used to assign the order type and sales area to the SAP customer number of the Sold-to party and the BuyerPartyID for the Supplier party.

EDSDC table: Customer-to-vendor assignment with sales organization details.

Order Type and Sales Area Determination by BuyerPartyID of Sold-to Party 

According to the code comments, this option is not recommended but is supported for compatibility purposes.

In this case, the Supplier Party section is not required. The order type and sales area are assigned to the Sold-to SAP customer number and the external number of the same Sold-to party.

Payload example: 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_2</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
    <OrderItem>

 

Required Settings:

  • The EDPAR table is used to map the BuyerPartyID to the SAP customer number. This record must be created for Sold-to determination.

EDPAR table: External-to-internal number mapping for sold-to party.

  • The EDSDC table is used to assign the order type and sales area to the SAP customer number of the Sold-to party and the BuyerPartyID for the same Sold-to party.

EDSDC table: Mapping customer to vendor with sales org and division details.

Order type and Sales area Determination: Error handling

If no record matching the inbound parameters is found, the following response is received:

Error: Sales area cannot be determined in SAP EDI response.

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" 
                         xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Sales area cannot be determined</text>
            <id>001(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>
